speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number four with Lipman Radzik. You were talking about what happened to you after liberation.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well after the liberation-- I have to-- to stress this very strongly-- there were a horrifying shock after where we were. So innocent victims of this horrifying chapter that the world, that the free world, will come to the rescue to us. They didn't. I walked around after the war in one shirt. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And blessed, my wife by that time, she was my only link to lifetime. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: I met her in-- in Theresienstadt.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was actually-- as I mentioned to you, a friend of mine from my city have said to me, I want to introduce to somebody, a very lovely girl. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I met her, and it was-- I was-- I must say, it was love at first sight. She was a tremendous inspiration for me, and she was a tremendous help to me, through all the years. By the way, if not her, I wouldn't be alive today. She was my mother. She was my girlfriend. She was everything what a human being. In other words, I-- I met her like somebody would walk in the Sahara Desert, without any water, without any to survival. She was my survival kit through the years.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we got married later on, in Italy. We was, after, transferred to Italy. And there-- I got married there, in Italy. This is a separate chapter from there. This-- but let me go back to, which I feel like that the world have really did not treat us as victims. Even after the war, we were put in-- in certain camps. The only thing which wasn't there were the German guards. And they treated us in a most inhumane way. There was no clothes. There was no food. There was no medical care. And it was terrible thing. And I remember, one time, a transfer come and brought clothes from America from there. And they brought in some old, donated clothes, which were there. And you were humiliated again. And they gave you a pair of paints, a pair of things. In other words, the suffering wasn't finished yet. And it was-- looked to me we didn't care anymore, even if you survived, or not survived, because nobody came to the rescue. It was the most horrifying thing. And I-- this-- this is a very blemish-- horrendous thing that the free world did not come to the rescue to us. And to be able to treat us back, to bring us back, to-- to humanity, to bring us back as human beings. You have to realize, there was many liberated from other nations-- Gentile people. Well, they all went back to their homes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were embraced. And they comfort them. I didn't have nowhere to go. I went back to my city. The house was there. All the other things was there. But all my loved ones is gone. And I was told by one of my neighbors, well, how come did you came back alive? That was the welcome what I come back to my own city. And as you know it, there was even pogroms after the war, in Poland, and other thing. Because they didn't believe it that someone will come back. They were afraid we will claim our homes and our businesses back. And we were, actually, for two years in displaced-- displaced persons camps, in Italy, and all over. We were in Germany. It was in Austria, and thing like that. And you were treated--

speaker: interviewee
text: I lived-- there was name of place called Cinecitta. Cinecitta means the house where you used to make those movies. And all what they have those-- where they make the movies. And we lived in the most horrifying things. There was no-- [INAUDIBLE] food there they were giving. There was terrible things. And they used to come from many other countries. Even our own people used to come there with cameras, and to show us. We-- I felt like that I am again in a zoo-- so humiliated. [INAUDIBLE] somebody once walked over to me and said to me, he wants to give me back a pair of socks, or some other thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't need those pair of socks. And I shove all it back. I don't want your socks. Someone wants to give me $1. I said, what do you think? I am-- there I'm still a human being. I said, I don't want nothing from you.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was a tremendous letdown. There was nowhere to go. And I feel like the world didn't give a hoot about us. I blame, many time, even, my own people, that they didn't come forth to it, the way it should be, like brothers and sisters. They didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go from Italy?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Italy, after being to 1947, I found out I had a cousin from my mother's side in Australia. And thanks to her, she sent me a permit-- [INAUDIBLE] a permit. And I went to Australia.

speaker: interviewee
text: One, my daughter, was born in Italy. And my son was born in Australia.

speaker: interviewee
text: My daughter's name is Hannah, but we call her Helen. And my son's name is Abraham, bless them both. They are tremendous. Mine son is the name after my father. And my daughter's name is named after my wife's-- her mother's name. And they're two adorable, two children. [INAUDIBLE] there is the two which keeps us alive. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you come to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came to New York. And I came in 1957, 4th of July.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do after you arrived here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I came in July. I was involved-- as a matter of fact, I had some good friends. And somebody came-- a very famous rabbi come, by the name of Rabbi Mordechai Kirschenbaum-- very well known, from the Mizrahi movement. And he came to Melbourne. And I said, I would like to go to America. Because Australia, by that time, wasn't so much developed. And I was-- want to go to America. Actually, I went to Australia via America. And I said, one day I shall return. [BACKGROUND NOISE] And he was the one who would send me all-- all papers to be able to come to America. And I had many congregations. And I was in the teaching field. I was in the rabbinical field. I was in many places in the states. And I must say, America was very good to me. And I'm very grateful with-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that I came here, to this land of freedom.

speaker: interviewee
text: My daughter lives in Israel, and my son lives in New Jersey.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my son is a very highly-learned man, a very wonderful human, a heart as gold. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He has the saying that there is-- the apple doesn't fall far from the tree. He has-- I once said to [INAUDIBLE], the-- the intelligence of the father, and the beauty and the goodness of the mother. There is a mixture of two beautiful things. We always [INAUDIBLE] ourselves, the good things, you know, it's from my side or from her side. And my daughter, Helen, bless her-- or our daughter, I should make it. Helen is a very beautiful human being. She has a beautiful voice. And she's a wonderful child. And she lives in Israel. I want to add now [INAUDIBLE] one thing, if I may. There one particular one, which I feel like they-- I owe this I should dwell a few minutes on them. Among the six million Jews were more than a million children. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The horrifying things what befell our people, those children, is so tragic, and so horrifying. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The terrible thing what happened to them, what they're-- have, according to the Germans, that-- that-- that they were born from Jewish parents. That was the only thing which happened. And those children cry out from the ashes of their horrifying death, that they were destroyed. The Germans were more vicious, much more, on the children, to destroy them first, that all the remnants of our faith should actually perish. And without children, no nation in the world could survive. That the way they were so vicious, especially on the children.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many grandchildren do you have?

speaker: interviewer
text: So with your children, and your grandchildren, they are eight people alive today, because you survived?

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think you survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I asked this question over and over again. Why me? Why not my brothers or my sisters? I felt like that I have a mission. I was designated to be able to sit here in front of you and tell to the world that what actually happened, that this horrible thing happened. And the words should be fulfill, never again, which have befell our people, without no reason whatsoever. And I feel like, to add on, that the free world stood by-- could have saved many, many of us. But they didn't give a hoot about it. They didn't care about it. And I feel like this is a tremendous, black chapter to the free world, and to Satan himself. Where was I, when I was in great need to save human beings which have been slaughtered, for the only one reason-- they were born Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would you like to say to future generations, and your grandchild, about your experience as a Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] unfortunately, this is 50 years after this terrible catastrophe, which have befell, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I would say, the whole world. Everybody is to blame. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Everybody. You cannot say, I wasn't there. You were there. Everybody there was there, and they could have helped. They didn't help. And this could have stopped, right from the beginning. But nobody cared. Nobody think well, it's only the Jews he's attacking. Or the other said, the other ones-- I wasn't there. And it-- I would like to leave that message to the entire free world. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And there is a very old saying yet, one that my professor said. If people do not learn from past history, they are liable to repeat the same mistake again. And according what I see what's going on now, I can see-- and I'm terribly shocked and disappointed that so little have learned from that catastrophe which has befell, actually, humanity.

speaker: interviewee
text: I may-- would like to add on [? noch-- ?] can I? I'd like to add on a few, especially, to give great-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'm greatly appreciated, and he inspired me quite a lot, when I went to see And this, even after 50 years, I have to say to Steven Spielberg, which his Hebrew name is Shmuel, and he remembers that word, Shmuel. I am hoping yet that I will have a chance to meet him in person. He had done something. And he was specially designated. And I feel like, in the bottom of my heart, from all our six million Jews, to represent them in this, to show to the world what actually took place. And I have to say to you, my dear Stephen, you didn't fail. You have done a tremendous job to show to the world. May the good lord bless you and keep you, you and your dear family, for that. It took some time, but you did it. And [HEBREW], may the lord protect you and guide you, and reward you what you have done. I also would like to give a special thanks to Mary Rothchild. She was a great inspiration. I have-- must give her tremendous courage to listening to those horrifying things, interviewing those survivors, that I feel like she was also specially designated, and has a part in her by fulfilling that great mitzvah, and that great deed. And you rest assured, dear Mary, you have find favor in mine eyes. By doing this, the good lord shall reward you, and fulfill you all the beautiful things which you so rightfully deserves. And finally, I wouldn't miss out those two beautiful people. That's Warren, doing a tremendous job, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Jaffa, for being a part in filming the things. May the good lord both bless you and give you all those what you heart desire. And keep doing that thing for many, many years to come. And I love you. You're both great. And you, again, Mary, you have done something beautiful. And I am very grateful to you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to introduce your wife to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I would like to introduce my beautiful wife. Her name is [? Gita, ?] and [? Gita ?] stands for the four of good. She's good things. She is good in her soul. She doesn't say much, but her goodness and her kindness usually taps it all and weighs it over the beautiful thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: You-- you have lived with your husband--

speaker: interviewer
text: You've lived with your husband for 50 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- would you like to say anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: About what? About my husband? Well, I can say-- I can return the same thing what my husband said to me. We are a very happy couple. We have a wonderful life for 50 years, [INAUDIBLE] all people, lived so happy, like we are.

speaker: interviewee
text: He's a wonderful person, and I think he did a wonderful job. And I am so sorry, Mary, that I promised you that I will do it, but I can't.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know what, that I very often ask my husband, have dream at night, and I scream. Because I cannot forget what I went through. So that's-- just listening to my husband, I got such a headache, and I went through so much. You have no idea. I cannot do it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I would love to do it. But I can't.

speaker: interviewer
text: I understand. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is my dear mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is Malka. Her maiden name is [? Zendal, ?] and she was born in Mlawa, Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think this picture was taken when-- right after her marriage.

speaker: interviewee
text: To be truthfully speaking, it's difficult for me to say.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, my dear mother was a great inspiration to be able to survive. She has instilled a lot of love and caring. She is a great inspiration. Even now, when I'm in a very sad, and finding it difficult to get by, her picture always inspires me to go on.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this picture the three of us-- my beautiful, good looking wife.

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is [? Gita, ?] and she is really good. She lives up to the name, which she has. And I think she looks like a film star. And I would have to remarry, I would marry her all over again.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the middle is someone-- we were a Bar Mitzvah in Savannah, Georgia. His name is Bert Tenenbaum, [INAUDIBLE] one of my pupils. And of course, that good looking man is yours truly.

speaker: interviewee
text: Name is Rabbi Lipman Radzik. And I've been in a number of congregations.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken maybe about-- in-- also in the '60s-- most probably in the '60s.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in-- I think it was in--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, not in Savannah, Georgia. I think it was taken in-- oh, I remember-- in a place which is called Long Branch, New Jersey.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is our dear son, Abraham Radzik. Very talented, and very beautiful human being. Does a lot of good things.

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture was taken I would say about 10 years ago.

speaker: interviewee
text: Those beautiful two people there in this pictures is my beautiful wife and my beautiful daughter.

speaker: interviewee
text: She is on the right. And she lives in Israel. And my wife's name is [? Gita. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken also maybe about a number of years ago.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nine years ago? OK, nine years ago.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my-- this is our great son, [? Dove. ?] He looks so cute, and he's beautiful. And we love him.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the son of mine son, Abraham.

speaker: interviewee
text: Picture was taken when he was maybe seven years old. Oh, about 15 years ago.

speaker: interviewee
text: Was-- that was in Kansas City. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is our grandson, Chaim Elbaum, which is mine daughter, who lives in Israel. And this was taken about-- I would say about 10 years ago.

speaker: interviewee
text: From the right, my beautiful wife. From the left, my granddaughter, Bathsheba, who live in Israel. And her name is Bathsheba.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the daughter of my daughter-- of our daughter.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken maybe about 10 years ago.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, from right, myself. In the middle is David Pra-- Prager-- very beautiful friend of ours. And he is the son of Dennis Prager, which is a very close friend of us. And he has been a great inspiration to us through the years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was the picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture was taken about three years ago, here in Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, and my wife there, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Here, in Los Angeles. Myself, Dennis Prager, and my beautiful wife, [? Gita. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a very close friend, Dr. Eric Roberts. He's our physician, and a tremendous friend to our family. We love him greatly.

speaker: interviewee
text: Picture was taken here in Los Angeles, in 1992.
